standards.rule: Every repo exposes the universal Justfile recipe names: setup, check, check-ci, format, lint, lint-ci, test, done. `check` fixes in place (local dev); `check-ci` is read-only (pre-commit hook + CI). Go implementations: format = `go fmt ./...`; lint = `go vet ./...` + `staticcheck ./...`; lint-ci additionally fails on `gofmt -l .` output; test = `go test ./...`.
standards.go_port_application: The Go port's Justfile must adopt these exact recipe names with the playbook's Go bodies. During the transition the repo is polyglot (Python + Go) — use the playbook's 'Mixed' pattern: each recipe runs both halves (ruff+pytest AND gofmt+vet+staticcheck+go test) until the Python side is retired. Add `staticcheck` and `go` to mise.toml. Note yolo-jail's current Justfile deviates slightly (check runs test-fast, has check-all); preserve the fast/full split when composing the Go recipes.

standards.rule: Go layout: `go.mod` at root, `cmd/` for binaries, `internal/` for private packages. `pkg/` only if there is a genuine public API surface — avoid `pkg/<name>/` for binary-only projects.
standards.go_port_application: yolo-jail is a binary-only CLI+entrypoint project: use `cmd/yolo/` (host CLI) and something like `cmd/yolo-entrypoint/`, with everything else under `internal/` (e.g. internal/config, internal/runtime, internal/shims, internal/agents). No `pkg/`. The two-binary split must mirror the current architecture: cli runs on host, entrypoint runs in-container and must stay dependency-light (the Python entrypoint is stdlib-only by design — the Go equivalent is a static binary with minimal/no third-party deps).

standards.rule: All logic is pure Python — no bash scripts with embedded heredocs. The only bash is generated *content* (shim scripts, .bashrc) written by the program. Use shlex.join-style quoting when passing commands to the container's `bash -c`.
standards.go_port_application: Translate directly: all logic in pure Go, no shell scripts in the repo, no heredoc-driven logic. Bash exists only as string content generated by Go (shims, bootstrap script, .bashrc). Port the quoting discipline with a Go shell-quoting equivalent of shlex.join (e.g. a vetted quoting function with tests) — this is a correctness-critical seam and deserves its own test module.

standards.rule: Commit straight to main with conventional commits (feat:, fix:, docs:, chore:, refactor:, test:). Commit every logical change immediately — never leave the working tree dirty, never batch unrelated changes. yolo-jail-specific reason: the nix image builds from the working tree, so dirty state causes confusing build/test failures. Run `just format` before committing; do NOT run `just check` before committing (the pre-commit hook runs `just check-ci`). If the hook rejects, fix and retry — never bypass with --no-verify.
standards.go_port_application: The porting agent commits after each module lands (one module or one coherent slice = one commit), on main, with conventional-commit messages scoped by area (e.g. `feat(go): port config merge`, `test(go): entrypoint shim generation`). Since the nix image builds from the working tree, an in-progress Go tree that breaks the build must never sit uncommitted while other work proceeds. Format via `go fmt`/`just format` first; let the pre-commit hook do verification.

standards.rule: Never `git commit --amend`. Always fix forward with a new commit — clean linear history where each commit is a distinct change.
standards.go_port_application: Absolute for the port: if a ported module has a bug found later, the fix is a new `fix(go): ...` commit, never an amend or rebase of the module's landing commit. This also means per-stage handoff docs should reference commit SHAs freely — they stay valid because history is never rewritten.

standards.rule: Canonical author on every commit: Matthew Schulkind <mschulkind@gmail.com>. No AI co-author trailers, no 'Generated with' footers, no robot-emoji footers — mechanically enforced by commit-msg and pre-push hooks that reject them.
standards.go_port_application: The porting agent must NOT append any Claude/AI attribution to commit messages (this overrides any harness default footer — the repo's hooks will reject such commits). Plain conventional-commit messages only.

standards.rule: Pick a track before starting. Light track (<3 files, clear scope): skip doc ceremony, inline notes in commits. Heavy track (multi-file, ambiguous, research-backed, high-risk): full structure — research → plan → tasks → implementation → QA, each with its own doc. Never switch tracks mid-task.
standards.go_port_application: A full Go port is unambiguously heavy-track: it needs a plan doc, per-stage task checklists, implementation logs, and QA docs before code starts. But each *individual stage* handed to the coding agent should be sized so its tasks are ≤1 hour each (docs/tasks granularity rule) — i.e. a stage is one subsystem (config loading, shim generation, container run/exec, briefing generation, port forwarding, etc.), decomposed into checklist items an Opus session can complete and commit incrementally. Don't hand the agent 'port the CLI' as one task.

standards.rule: Every plan/research log/task list maintains an Open Questions section; active questions sync to project-root OPEN_QUESTIONS.md. Format: `### Question title as a sentence fragment`, context paragraph, `_Leaning:_ <best guess + rationale>`, then `**Answer:**` followed by an empty blockquote the user fills in. Answered questions move to an 'Answered' section — never deleted (they become decision records).
standards.go_port_application: The port plan and every stage doc carry this exact Open Questions block. Port-shaped questions (e.g. 'which JSONC parser', 'single binary vs two binaries', 'how to replace typer', 'keep setuptools-scm vs goreleaser tags during transition') get a stated Leaning so the human can approve by filling the blockquote asynchronously. Blocked stages list their blocking question in root OPEN_QUESTIONS.md so the human sees it without opening stage docs.

standards.rule: Research is cumulative: read docs/research/ before any web search; investigate only the gaps; end every round by updating the evergreen domain doc (docs/research/<DOMAIN>.md), not just a timestamped log. Skip web search entirely if the KB already answers the question.
standards.go_port_application: Before porting each subsystem, the agent reads the existing docs (/workspace/docs/*.md are dense with load-bearing behavior contracts — e.g. agent-briefings.md documents inode-preservation semantics that MUST survive the port). Findings about Python-behavior-to-preserve go into an evergreen doc (e.g. docs/research/go-port-parity.md) so later stages don't rediscover them.

standards.rule: When modifying src/cli/ or src/entrypoint/, ALWAYS verify with a nested jail (`yolo -- bash` from inside the jail) before telling the human to test on the host. Container startup errors (mount failures, permission errors, read-only fs conflicts) are only caught by actually running the container — unit tests are not sufficient. cli changes take effect on next `yolo` invocation (no rebuild); entrypoint/flake.nix changes require `just load && just install` on the host because the entrypoint is baked into the Nix image.
standards.go_port_application: This is the port's core verification gate. Every stage that touches the Go equivalents of cli or entrypoint code ends with an actual nested-jail launch exercising the changed path (start container, check mounts/shims/briefings/forwarded ports), not just `go test`. The stage's definition-of-done includes 'nested jail verified' with the command and observed output recorded in the stage's implementation log. Stages changing the Go entrypoint must flag in the handoff doc that the human needs `just load && just install` on the host before host-side testing.

standards.rule: After EVERY edit to yolo-jail.jsonc or the user config, run `yolo check` (or `yolo check --no-build` inside a running jail) before asking for a restart. Config changes between startups show a normalized diff and require human y/N approval — agents can edit config but humans must approve; agents must self-validate.
standards.go_port_application: Two implications: (1) as workflow — if the porting agent needs Go toolchain packages in the jail, it edits yolo-jail.jsonc `packages`, runs `yolo check`, and asks the human to restart; (2) as porting scope — the config-snapshot/diff/approval machinery, normalized-config comparison, and `yolo check` probes are behavior contracts the Go port must reproduce exactly, including the non-interactive accept-with-warning path. docs/config-safety.md is the spec.

standards.rule: For any capability, pick ONE happy-path tool that covers the whole support matrix; alternatives are at most prose escape hatches. Rank by matrix coverage, least per-user infrastructure, consistency, one-time maintainer cost, minimal support surface. `yolo check` and CLI errors name THE fix, never a menu. Tooling (just recipes, CI, defaults) is wired for the one path.
standards.go_port_application: Governs port decisions: one Go build path (e.g. `go build` via just recipes — don't also support make/bazel/goreleaser-local-builds as co-equal options); one dependency approach; one way to ship the entrypoint binary into the image. Ported error messages must keep the 'name THE path' style — when translating Python error/check strings, resist enumerating alternatives. Also biases toward Go's actual strength here: a single static binary is itself the happy-path win (no uv/venv/shebang-patching machinery), so the port should delete the relocatable-editable-install complexity in the current Justfile setup recipe rather than translate it.

standards.rule: Language-agnostic floor every repo keeps: Justfile, mise.toml (pinned toolchains), LICENSE (Apache 2.0), README.md, AGENTS.md (repo-specific guidance only, no injected shared block), CLAUDE.md as a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import, .gitignore (project-relevant patterns only; maintainer-local ignores in .git/info/exclude), .github/workflows/ci.yml running `just check-ci`, .github/dependabot.yml (weekly, per-ecosystem + github-actions).
standards.go_port_application: The port happens in-place in the existing repo, so: add `gomod` to dependabot.yml alongside pip/github-actions; pin Go in mise.toml; keep CI on `just check-ci` (which grows the Go half); update AGENTS.md's architecture sections as modules move from src/*.py to Go (AGENTS.md must describe the polyglot structure so newcomers don't trip — playbook polyglot rule). Also note current drift: /workspace/CLAUDE.md is empty rather than `@AGENTS.md` — fix as a docs: commit.

standards.rule: Go projects: git tags are the version source of truth (goreleaser reads them); required channels are Homebrew tap (goreleaser `brews:` writes Formula/<name>.rb), `go install github.com/mschulkind-oss/<name>@latest`, and source (`git clone` + `mise install` + `just install`). Tag format v<major>.<minor>.<patch>. README `## Install` section before Usage in the required format. ci.yml + release.yml (+publish.yml, may be unified under goreleaser).
standards.go_port_application: End-state standard for when the Go port becomes the shipping artifact: replace setuptools-scm/PyPI with tag-driven goreleaser, wire the tap formula update into release.yml, ensure `go install ...@latest` works from the module path, and rewrite README's Install section. Not needed per-stage — this is the final 'cutover' stage of the port plan, and any deviation (e.g. keeping PyPI during transition) gets recorded per distribution.md's Deviations rule.

standards.rule: At end of session run `just done` (where it exists) and report output; end-of-task `git status` must be clean. Private notes/research go in swarf/, not the public repo.
standards.go_port_application: yolo-jail's Justfile has no `done` recipe today — the porting agent's equivalent end-of-stage ritual is: `just format`, commit, working tree clean, nested-jail verification recorded, handoff doc updated. Anything personal/private discovered during the port does not get committed to the repo.

standards.rule: Never `rm` — move deleted files to `trash/` (local-only, never committed). `scratch/` is the git-tracked working surface for notes that must survive a context reset. New directories require a crisp answer to 'how is this different from an existing one' — default to scratch/, don't invent subdirs.
standards.go_port_application: As Python modules are retired stage-by-stage, they are deleted in a normal `refactor(go): remove ported src/cli/foo.py` commit (git history is the recovery net for tracked files; trash/ is for untracked work). The porting agent keeps its cross-session working state in scratch/ or the stage docs — never in loose root-level files.

standards.source: /workspace/AGENTS.md (§Testing Guidelines) — the No Agent Tests rule
standards.rule: Automated tests must NOT run copilot/gemini/claude interactively — version checks only, never interactive sessions or API calls. Agent functionality is tested manually (`yolo -- claude` etc.) before committing.
standards.go_port_application: Go integration tests inherit this constraint verbatim: tests may assert binaries exist and configs are generated correctly, but a `go test` must never spawn an agent session. Stages touching agent-facing surfaces (briefing generation, MCP/LSP config, settings.json) end with a manual `yolo -- claude` smoke test noted in the stage log.

standards.rule: Long QA passes needing staged human review are chunked into docs/qa/batch-<N>.md files; present one batch at a time, wait for the human to update statuses, then process.
standards.go_port_application: Use for the port's parity-verification passes: e.g. a batch file per subsystem listing behavior contracts (from the docs and Python tests) with pass/fail status the human can review incrementally, rather than one monolithic 'does the Go version match' review at the end.

docs_layout:
Standard layout (agent-standards references/doc-layout.md, mirrored in new-project references/standard-layout.md): docs/research/ (investigation logs YYYY-MM-DD_<topic>.md AND evergreen domain docs — every research round ends with the domain doc updated), docs/plans/ (in-flight RFCs; shipped plans migrate their decisions to design/ and don't linger), docs/design/ (one ADR-style file per durable decision: Status/Context/Decision/Consequences), docs/tasks/ (checklists for active work, each item ≤1 hour, archived when merged), docs/implementation/ (execution logs from heavy-track work: commands run, output captures), docs/qa/ (test plans, batch-<N>.md files, reports), docs/guides/ (durable how-tos, not in-flight notes). scratch/ (git-tracked notes) and trash/ (never-committed soft deletes) are siblings of docs/, never under it. When in doubt, scratch/ — new subdirs need a crisp differentiator.

Where the Go port artifacts live: the port PLAN is an RFC → docs/plans/go-port.md (with the Open Questions block, module inventory, stage ordering, and cutover criteria; on completion its decisions migrate to docs/design/go-port.md as an ADR). Per-stage docs: task checklists in docs/tasks/go-port-stage-<N>-<subsystem>.md (≤1h items); execution/verification logs in docs/implementation/go-port-stage-<N>.md; parity QA batches in docs/qa/go-port-batch-<N>.md. CAVEAT — the yolo-jail repo currently uses a flat docs/ with a handoff-<topic>.md convention (docs/handoff-cachix-cache.md, handoff-macos-*.md, etc.) and docs/runbooks/, not the standard subdirs. Per-stage HANDOFF docs (context the next Opus session needs: what landed, what's verified, what's next, blocking questions) should follow the repo's live convention — docs/handoff-go-port-stage-<N>.md — unless the human approves migrating docs/ to the standard layout first; raise that as an Open Question in the plan rather than unilaterally restructuring docs/. Active blocking questions additionally sync to a root OPEN_QUESTIONS.md.
testing_standards: Two-tier test split (workspace AGENTS.md §Testing Guidelines + Justfile): FAST tests — unit only, no containers — run by the pre-commit hook via `just check-ci` (Python side: pytest -m "not slow" -n 4 --dist worksteal; parallelism capped at 4, and the slow suite stays serial because session-scoped image build/load must never run per-worker). FULL tests — container integration — run by GitHub CI (`just test` / `check-all`). The Go port must mirror this: fast `go test ./...` (with `-short` or a build tag excluding container tests) wired into check-ci, container-touching Go integration tests in the full/CI tier only. Entrypoint config-generation logic (shims, MCP/LSP configs, bashrc, briefings) is unit-testable without containers — the Python tests/test_entrypoint.py pattern is the model: the Go entrypoint must keep pure generation functions separable from container execution so parity can be asserted golden-file style against the Python outputs during transition. Hard rules: automated tests must NEVER run copilot/gemini/claude interactively or make API calls (--version checks only); agent functionality is verified manually (`yolo -- claude` etc.) before committing. Unit tests are explicitly NOT sufficient for cli/entrypoint changes — container startup errors (mounts, permissions, read-only fs) only surface in a real nested-jail run, so every stage's definition-of-done includes an actual `yolo -- bash` (or targeted `yolo -- <cmd>`) exercising the changed path. Lint gate for Go per the playbook: go vet + staticcheck, gofmt-clean enforced in lint-ci; treat this as the warnings-as-errors equivalent of the Rust clippy -D warnings stance. CI keeps running `just check-ci`, which grows the Go half alongside the Python half (Mixed-recipe composition) until cutover.
workflow_standards:
COMMIT DISCIPLINE: commit straight to main; conventional commits (feat/fix/docs/chore/refactor/test, scoped e.g. `feat(go): ...`); commit IMMEDIATELY after every logical change without being asked — on yolo-jail this is load-bearing because the nix image builds from the working tree (AGENTS.md §Committing item 5 + memory). NEVER amend — always fix forward with a new commit (hard user requirement, memory/feedback_no_amend.md). Run `just format` before committing; do NOT run `just check` (redundant — the pre-commit hook runs `just check-ci`); if the hook rejects, fix and retry, never --no-verify. No AI attribution trailers or generation footers of any kind — canonical author Matthew Schulkind <mschulkind@gmail.com>, mechanically enforced by commit-msg/pre-push hooks (this overrides any harness default commit footer). Repo AGENTS.md says 'always commit and push' for this project since the image builds from the tree; jail sessions lack host credentials, so if push fails, note it for the human rather than treating it as done. End of task: git status clean.

VERIFICATION: any change to src/cli/ or src/entrypoint/ (or their Go successors) MUST be verified end-to-end in a nested jail (`yolo -- bash` from inside the jail) before telling the human to test on the host — unit tests alone never suffice because mount/permission/startup failures only appear when the container actually runs. cli-side changes go live on the next `yolo` invocation; entrypoint/flake.nix changes require the human to run `just load && just install` on the host — each stage handoff must state which of these applies. After every yolo-jail.jsonc edit: `yolo check` (`--no-build` in-jail) before requesting a restart. Record the verification commands + observed output in the stage's implementation/handoff doc.

OPEN QUESTIONS (agent-standards format, mandatory in the plan and every stage doc): `### <question as sentence fragment>` / context paragraph / `_Leaning:_ <best guess + rationale>` / `**Answer:**` + empty blockquote for the human to fill; answered questions move to an Answered section, never deleted; active questions sync to root OPEN_QUESTIONS.md. Blocked stages surface their question there and proceed on non-blocked work.

SCOPE SIZING: the port is heavy-track (research → plan → tasks → implementation → QA, each with docs) — pick the track up front, never switch mid-task. Stage = one subsystem (config load/merge+check, shim generation, briefing generation, container run/exec/reuse, port forwarding, cgroup daemon, loopholes, etc.), sized so an Opus session can land it with tasks of ≤1 hour each, committing incrementally and ending with a nested-jail verification and a handoff doc. Do not hand an agent 'port the CLI' as a single unit; do not let a stage span a session boundary without a written handoff.
notes: Source files read in full: /home/agent/.claude/skills/agent-standards/SKILL.md + references/doc-layout.md; /home/agent/.claude/skills/new-project/SKILL.md + references/{playbook.md via open-source-project copy, standard-layout.md, tech-stack.md}; /home/agent/.claude/skills/open-source-project/SKILL.md + references/{playbook.md, commit-attribution.md, distribution.md, agents-md-block.md}; /workspace/AGENTS.md; /workspace/CLAUDE.md (EMPTY — drift vs the one-line @AGENTS.md standard, worth a docs: fix commit); /workspace/docs/{happy-path-principle.md, config-safety.md, agent-briefings.md}; /workspace/Justfile; memory files feedback_no_amend.md and feedback_commit_after_each_change.md. Referenced-but-absent files: playbook.md points at agent-standards.md and cross-repo-templates.md which are not present in the skill dirs (only the sections quoted in the playbook are available). Two conflicts resolved in favor of user standards: (1) the harness's default Claude co-author commit footer is forbidden by commit-attribution.md and blocked by hooks; (2) yolo-jail's docs/ is flat (handoff-*.md convention) rather than the standard subdir layout — recommendation is to follow the live repo convention for handoffs and raise layout migration as an Open Question rather than restructuring unilaterally. Also load-bearing for the port: docs/agent-briefings.md documents inode-preservation refresh semantics (write_text truncate-in-place, never unlink-and-recreate) explicitly marked as a silent-breakage trap — the Go port must preserve it and its warning comment. The distribution.md scope table classifies yolo-jail as 'Python CLI + container'; a Go port shifts it to the Go channel set (goreleaser, go install, tap formula) — record the transition plan in the cutover stage and any interim deviation per distribution.md's Deviations rule.
